author: @boblewis5558
text:
I 100% agree Robert! The house where we now live (for five years) had a solar installation of the usual nominal 3kW (2.88kw rated) and which the previous owners wanted to take away if we didn't pay an additional £5k!! We refused as we could buy a brand new installation for just over £3k with more efficient panels and a brand new 10 year warranty. They succumbed as they realised just how much it would cost them to de-install/re-install and we paid just £2k ... A bargain as it transpires.

In the five years we've lived here, with the FIT agreement they had in place that £2k has been paid back at least TWICE. Our production over those five years, as evidenced by the historical data right out of the inverter memory via bluetooth has meant a typical annual payback of around £1600-1700! So our annual energy bill is very low (no payback tariff for the gas!)

Just ONE problem with most such systems installed in the UK is that despite producing MW hours of energy our consumption of that energy is limited to the real time output, which on a bright but overcast day is only about 350-600 watts! But on that same bright but overcast day our kWh generated in Summer will often be over 10kWh for the day. That's  over HALF our daily usage!

The solution is obvious ... We need to store ALL of it, export NONE of it and use ALL of it ourselves. This is especially true since we STILL will be paid for generating that amount (great tariff - over £800 for just ONE summer quarter's production!). Battery storage is the answer PLUS an inverter capable of delivering electric oven, dishwasher, washing machine, tumble drier and fridge/freezer power ... The current one can only do 3kW!

Batteries first though and a low cost off-grid inverter initially feeding fridge freezer and lighting circuits.

It's easier to dope. It's easier to handle. So why are the brain ache's still sticking to using the wrong data from 50 years ago?

author: @MikeAG333
text:
The energy payback period was not a myth until very recently. I'm an English architect, and about 8 or 9 years ago I did a study for a project using the manufacturer's own figures for the embodied energy in their PV arrays. In the particular location we were looking at (Midlands, UK), it was going to take 23 years to generate the same amount of electricity as had gone into their manufacture. The panels had an estimated lifespan of 25 years. So, please don't describe this issue as a myth.

The fact is that things are very much better these days, and panels placed in that location today might well have an energy payback period of only 3 or 4 years. (It wasn't a perfect location, suffering a modicum of late afternoon overshadowing). We also are on the cusp of much improved performance from radically different new types of solar panels which are supposed to change the equation radically.

Yes, more sunshine is better for making solar energy.
But don't make the mistake of thinking that solar isn't as good in the winter when it's sunny, but not warm out. Solar panels get better in the cold, if you keep the snow off.

In highly populated areas mostly used natural gas (5-7 rubles per cubic meter) and you can get not only electricity but heat from it at the same time.

replies.author: @AnalystPrime
replies.text:
@АндрейПолитов-щ8г You could rely on solar, it would just take enough energy storage or lots of production and long distance power lines. Using a mix is smarter so you don't depend on one, better to have extra capacity than a blackout. Burning oil or gas is easier for producing large amounts of heat, but by using renewables for electricity you can save that fuel for the colder seasons.

The problem with fossil fuels is that the low price is not going to stay low. Either the government adds taxes to fix the problems caused by pollution(or simply out of greed), the company finds a better customer who would pay more so they raise the price, or the easily accessed source that allows for such low prices dries up and production ends or becomes far more expensive.

author: @TerryGilsenan
text:
There is a simpler payback algorithm: Cost.

Its like this:

The cost of the PV panels & the installation & All costs to the point of energy production = Input.
The savings provided from all other forms of energy, by the utilization of this installation = output.

For me the cost was most recently around AUD$7k as the input cost, and the output value was approximately AUD$160/month

This would calculate to a break even at (7000/160) months, approximately 43.75 months, or just less than 4 years.

The reason I would use cost rather than energy as the input, is that the business processes int he manufacturing, shipping, and distribution chain, would cover ALL the energy inputs and convert them into an easily calculable metric: Money.

The output metric is also best done this way, as kJ, Wh, Amps etc, are cumbersome to work with, when all my current energy is already metered on the same metric: Money.

I know what the PV array cost in dollars, and I know exactly what it saved me (in my case, it saved me 100%) compared to my previous energy costs.

replies.author: @TerryGilsenan
replies.text: @binomalia Not really appropriate for mine as I don't have batteries. The PV is glass and aluminium, so that will have a net return value at the recycle point.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: mate - I get your argument - but you can bet your bottom dollar energy payback would still come up - it's really why I didn't approach it from that stand point because all the comments I got weren't about the economics but about that energy payback

author: @yougeo
text: Remember also there is a whole world of common devices that are designed to run directly off 12 volts that are designed for the trucking and the RV industry and to a lesser extent the Marine industry. You can go to many truck stops and get 12 volt refrigerators and other things therefore bypassing the need to have an inverter which then puts power into the refrigerator which then down converts that usually back into a DC voltage source. And all that waste electricity. So if you can buy an appliance like a 12 volt DC refrigerator designed for a truck driver then you can hook that right up to your solar panels with a tiny interim battery between the two and have that thing run all day to keep cool and if it's well insulated refrigerator it'll stay cool overnight when you're not opening the door anyway and it'll be ready in the morning to experience another cool down.

replies.author: @Gilespargiter
replies.text: I do actually totally agree with you. Only trucks are pretty invariably 24V

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: yeah - I really think that is the way to go - I was pondering this to be honest

author: @yougeo
text: One other point I forgot to mention. The battery worries are also way overstated. The truth is you don't need nearly as many batteries as people talk about if you have a lot of solar panels. For an example I ran a trolling motor off a 6 amp hour battery directly connected to about 300 watts of solar panels. That trolling motor was able to run on medium speed all day long using that little tiny battery as basically a capacitor to fill in when a cloud came over the sun. So in reality now that the solar wattage has become so cheap you no longer have to have a giant battery Bank to run things. You can use a tiny interim battery as almost a capacitor and in fact I did try a capacitor and it sort of worked but couldn't take the real lows out. But you can use this tiny batteries and you don't need a lot of batteries unless what you're trying to do is run something overnight. But 90% of what you need to do you don't need to run overnight. If you get yourself some solar powered lights the lights are independent of any battery system that you have. If you heat a big tank of water during the day with your solar panel then that hot water will radiate heat throughout your house all night and you don't need to run any electric heater at night. So the big takeaway is the battery problem is a non-issue if you design around it and you get more solar wattage and just use the batteries not as a primary power source but as a interim power source like a capacitor.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I am with you mate - there are just so many ways to go - for example you could use your water tank as a dump load! why just think batteries - it amazes me really

author: @kilterman2000
text:
I can agree that this myth may not be true.  As far as repaying itself in just a couple years I am unsure.  I think if you were to include all the installation costs it will take longer to get your money back.  Especially if you could not install the system yourself.  I live in the the US and was quoted a 15 kw system with installation of $30,000 US.  If you pay all that it would take many years to pay itself off if you can only save a couple hundred a month.  In addition, I live in an area where it is cloudy almost 80% of the year.  With that being said I was told I more panels to cover in this area.  I consider myself fairly handy so I would be able to do most of the installation myself, once I got the guts to pull the trigger on a system that is.  I would love to use alternative energy.  I have been looking into this for over 10 years and cannot find the best type of system for me.   I love your channel.  I think the best way for me to go would be to use many smaller different ideas you present to help cover when the sun is not out.  

Thanks again for all the great content.

author: @simongross3122
text:
It's true that the payback period has shifted from the early days of solar and I think it is still shifting.  It is also different by location.  

Here in Sydney Australia, we actually have electricity companies trying to sell us home solar with or without a battery.  I expect they are making a healthy profit from the installation and supply.  

The feed-in tariffs are pretty poor, but I suspect that they actually don't want too much fed in since they are still trying to sell us their own electricity generated from coal, and also they are trying to limit feed-ins to avoid voltage spikes and harmonic distortion from destabilising the grid.   They even tell you the payback period assuming current electricity prices.  

The payback period for my own house would be close to 10 years with solar plus a battery or 5 years without a battery because batteries are still very expensive.  At my age it doesn't seem worth it, but perhaps with more competition it will become more attractive.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I think you are talking about economic payback mate not energy payback

author: @johnwest7993
text:
My own little diatribe:
A kW of sunlight hits every square meter of ground. So what if you did use more of that energy to create a PV panel than the panel will produce as electricity? You weren't using that energy for anything anyway. And in the end you get it all back as the same amount of heat as the sun would have put into that square meter of ground. All we're really discussing is the utility of our energy sources, and changing some of it to some form we need from some form we don't. Iceland uses geothermal electricity to make more aluminum there than all the aluminum made in the USA, because the electricity there is cheap and plentiful. The question isn't about how MUCH electricity is needed to make the aluminum. It's about the COST of it. Why not use the same standard for PV panels?

The payback period for my panels (and inverter and batteries,) was about 6 weeks, since they were essentially what allowed me to live in my motorhome off the grid for 2 years while I was doing R&D work, and saving over $1000 per month in rent. And while I sold the RV I still have the panels sitting in my closet, which are now essentially free for the next project. Payback period, zero. 

The 'all things being equal' argument is simply silly. Those who set up that argument are stacking the deck against PV. All things are NOT equal. The cost and value relationship of any PV installation depends almost entirely on the individual circumstances of that installation. Calculate the payback period for grid power vs PV power for a cabin in the mountains that is 5 miles from the grid, or for a place in the desert that's 10 miles from it, and all of a sudden PV's pay for themselves in a real hurry. Or calculate the value of a PV system when a winter  dumps 3 feet of snow and takes out the grid power for a week. I figure my PV system paid for itself that week. 

People who make those foolish arguments are just mouthing nonsense that was once talking points produced by oil companies when they were, and still are, trying to fight off alternative and renewable energy. They are clearly failing. But thank you for helping to drive a dagger through their evil hearts. It's a real public service.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: you make good points mate - cheers

author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
text:
B. I was saying in my deleted comment also, that I really have never concerned myself with payback in money, but rather how long to recoup the amount of energy it took to make the panel, and hopefully have a way we can use solar panels to generate the power used to make more solar panels either from raw or recycled materials and that amount be more solar panels than used to run the facility, 

if you are worried about cost in money vs return I would say do what I did and look for ways to get them cheaper, like my solar cells I got for about 9 cents per watt because when packed up they were not stacked perfectly and cot tiny chips on the edges, that while it did not effect their function nor the time to set up a solar panel, it did make them look a little uglier, and thus saved me about 90 cents to several dollars per watt in the cost of them :)  (ie I paid like 90 bucks for what was supposed to be 1000 watts worth though really after tallying I found I have closer to 1700-2000 watts worth of solar cells in that package so...) XD

A. I thought silicon solar cells were rated to degrade in power output by .5% of the original output so 1 year in 100 watt unit puts out 99.5watts and year two it now puts out 99 watts so theoretically it should still be putting out half a watt 199 years after installed, so I am guessing the reason for 40 year mark is due to other things like seals and such failing, (silicon rubber they seal them with I think is probably where that 25 year warranty would be bad if longer since that stuff is usually rated for 25 years but I would guess if you just went out and reapplied that to all your panels at year 20 or so you would get another boost of about 25 years of protection. and so on.

author: @yougeo
text: Another dimension today of solar power is you can get a lot of devices that are actually solar powered themselves. For example you can buy off the shelf lights for landscaping that are solar powered it will recharge themselves everyday and run for a good four or five hours every night. So you don't need a main system panel Network to plug those into recharge them. You can buy a small panel to recharge your phone and dedicated to that. You can buy lights that have their own solar cells on them that we charge themself so you don't have to plug into any kind of electric system. So there are many of the reasons that people have electricity to begin with such as light and recharging electrical devices that you don't need a man's system for at all. Just look for the items that recharge themselves.

replies.text: I think you have put your finger on it mate - decentralisation down to the lowest common factor - Nice

author: @yougeo
text: All I know is the $300 I spent for some fold-out panels on a boat put out up to 14 amps in good sunlight and allow me complete Independence on the boat and I don't care how much energy it took to make the panels and the cost to reserve ratios or any of the other nonsense. All I know is it makes me a completely independent person on a boat when I want to be. It is a life changer. And that's true with most solar panels. And now you can buy used solar panels on the market. I bought 2240 watt hard framed glass faced panels coming out of an old solar farm that are only 13% efficient and I got the whole thing for $550 delivered to my door. So now I have thousands of watts of Independence and I don't care what it costs or what the recycling was or what the ratios are but it makes me truly independent and that is a priceless benefit. I can run my coffee maker I can run my air conditioner I can run my refrigerator I can charge my phone I can do everything I want to do even if I can't do it all at the same time but I can do it all and be like an Apollo astronaut and be able to go around the Moon completely independent of the Earth because I have a simple reliable power source that will probably last longer than my lifetime. People who get lost in the calculations of solar panels miss the point the point is it's free energy from the sky that makes you independent. And it's priceless.

replies.author: @DanielSMatthews
replies.text: Small scale or remote opportunistic energy harvesting is the ideal niche for solar.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: you put that really nicely mate and it is - to me - the whole point

replies.author: @yougeo
replies.text: @Frank Blackcrow the reliance on the battery issue really misses the point. When you have enough wattage on your solar panels you can use a tiny 6 amp hour battery in between the panels and whatever you want to run. The item you want to run will drive itself directly from the power with a filtering effect from the battery. So you're barely using the battery. You're not even discharging the battery. And it just is acting like sort of a big super capacitor that can handle any little independent surges that might come from cloud cover or something like that. Once you do that and once you realize that the whole thing about having a bunch of batteries that are going to wear out after so many life cycles is a non-issue.

replies.author: @brianjones8673
replies.text: @Frank Blackcrow that also depends on the battery how durable or how much capacity. Iron phosphate for example has less capacity than the newer chemistry batteries, but they have been demonstrated to be capable of living longer than most humans. I personally am interested in some of the aluminum based batteries that seem sub par on performance but easily maintained and repaired.

author: @MacsMachines

replies.author: @DanielSMatthews
replies.text: Fossil fuel is captured sunlight via photosynthesis and the maximum theoretical efficiency of the photosynthesis process is approximately 11% so you need a decade of growth for 1 year of burning, to put it simplistically. What this does highlight is that we are consuming a resource that takes far longer to accumulate than it will take us to exhaust it, and therein lies the most important risk with fossil fuels. We could never replace them with biomass at the rate we use them, in fact given that solar is less than 3 times more efficient that photosynthesis we can't use solar to replace fossil fuel either. We either reduce our energy consumption globally by an order of magnitude, and see growth collapse, or we go nuclear.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate

replies.author: @Gilespargiter
replies.text:
@DanielSMatthews What peculiar logic you ascribe to. 
Actually we can use solar to replace our use of fossil fuels, because we have plenty more than enough sunshine not being used for photosynthesis landing on the planet.

replies.author: @DanielSMatthews
replies.text: @Gilespargiter Solar is an opportunistic power source, it can never be a safe base load supply. Read the recent article in Nature, we have a 1 in 6 chance of a mega volcano eruption this century, and that will bring a volcanic winter, solar will then be useless. Billions will die. Research "536 the worst year to be alive" if you want to learn a few lessons via the historical record.

Well if the Solar System only has about a 40 year lifespan all this carbon footprint is a bit of a waste of time! ;o)

author: @aaronfranklin324
text:
A friend of mine has 6 ex ministry of transport solar panels built in Australia in 1977. They all still produce 3.7 amps at 16 volts (their 60W rating) and are the same area as my new 50w panels.
And that was when I tested them in early July, late afternoon in midwinter Tauranga NZ.

It has me questioning everything we have been told about increasing efficiency in recent years.
They need no charge controllers because the voltage is ideal for LA batteries, and if you never use inverters you get at least ten up to 100 times as much Work with 12V LED lights, brushed DC 18V powertools, and laptop or phone charging systems.
A 100W 18V brushed DC skill saw is just as powerful as a 1000W 240AC model the same size.
When run on 24V Lead Acid car batteries, much lighter, quieter, no vibration.
We don't need grid or electronics, and if you require your house for 12/24V DC then you don't need electricians, inspections....
And you can even sell electricity to your neighbors.
If you occasionally need 240VAC, use a cheap portable inverter.

Murray, how about a video  showing how to bypass the ridiculously inefficient power supply in a modern TV, and wire it for low voltage DC?
I'm quite sure you would reduce its power consumption to 10% of what you use plugging in to the mains.

replies.author: @Teknopottu
replies.text:
Great stuff! Many appliances can be used with battery and solar power with no transformers in between. Kept my drinks cool at summer with 20yo car fridge connected straight into a solar panel. 

A bit doubtfull on the claim that transformers waste 90% of the power, though.

replies.author: @ancapftw9113
replies.text: @Teknopottu yeah, they are probably 90% or 80% efficient.  So you will save some energy going directly to DC, but not 90%.

replies.author: @stuffoflardohfortheloveof
replies.text: That’s really interesting……cmon Rob, there’s an interesting project (says he sat on his a*se) 👍

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I think that is an awesome story mate - and I agree if you move to 12 or24V DC system no need for an inverter - there is a huge saving right there

replies.author: @aaronfranklin324
replies.text:
@Teknopottu not transformers. AC-DC electronic converters.
Remember, if they tell you something is "up to 90% efficient" in the marketing, then it isn't a lie if it never exceeds 20 percent efficiency.
I had a laptop that drew 250w. 
By cutting off the power supply and wiring it direct to 12V lead acid batteries, it only used 20W.
Look at the input voltage and amps on a mains phone charger, and the output volts and amps, you might be quite shocked.
Some DC/DC converter circuits can be theoretically pretty efficient. Like Buck-Boost for example. But in practice you need expensive components to get any where near theoretical.
And cheap nasty is the commercial rule.

author: @SundogbuildersNet
text:
My little cabin is powered with a micro solar system. To keep costs down and bang for the buck up, I'm not inverting, just running 12 volt DC inside. Fine for lighting and charging devices. The wifi router is 12v DC too, I just turn it on when i want it 'cause it's a lot on this tiny system. 

I really love this system but my biggest issues are always with the damn batteries. Definately the weak link in off grid living!

Your offerings always get me thinking and often inspire my own nutty ideas. Thanks for what you do!

author: @rogerbraintree9552
text: Well I commented in the earlier solar myths video that very myth of it taking more energy to make than what you get back.  It was probably true  back in the victorian times when the photo electric effect was discovered using selenium, and perhaps for a few decades thereafter and on to 1960.  But at some point they must have crossed that threshold and began to generate more energy than it took to manufacture.  With that myth debunked pv solar panels look like a good bet.

author: @pseudoname9229
text: What if the solar irradiance is critically low in the area they are implemented? Some northern European countries are not very sunny. What if the resources that make the panels, like copper, lithium, and cobalt, are becoming increasingly scarce due to demand? Maybe those metals could have been used for better alternatives, like wind turbines. It’s said that in order to meet the goals of net neutral carbon emissions by 2050 we must exhume 1.5x the amount of copper ever mined. Does it not require increasing amounts of energy to extract these resources? Heck, Lithium is up 400% year-over-year. It’s not a myth that some areas are better for solar than others, and I would wager that there are some areas where it just isn’t worth it.

author: @551moley
text: It can come down to simple maths, The cost for me to get  electricity was £11,000 plus, for a similar sum I've got a system that   provides 100% of my needs for 8 or 9 months a year without the generator (average 5 to 6 kwh per day) for winter the generator runs for 2 or 3 hours a couple of times a week normally at the same time as the washer and dryer, red diesel cost around £120 @ 80p litre so that will go up to something like £180, still roughly the cost of the standing charge. I had a few family members pretty much laugh at me for being too tight to pay £11,000, now after 3 years without a bill or lack of power they have changed their tone probably due to them just paying a £700 bill for 3 months power, I had to smile and tell him he needs some solar 😉.

author: @Gilespargiter
text:
Brilliant video. I recently made a quite long post in answer to someone on Oddysee. I thought I remembered the energy payback at 3-5years on solar, obviously I'm out of date.
Next; the old chestnut about bird strike and wind turbines. I also used quite old figures I have remembered from a report a few years back about bird deaths due to human activity. First, domestic cats - about 350/year each. Long way second, motor vehicles and around the same, power lines. Then windows, then way, way below that wind turbines. However I have become aware of researhc that shows that birds as sentient beings learn about wind turbines and the bird strike rate, which is very low anyway, becomes even lower on established turbines. Got the latest figures?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I don't mate - I will have to looking it because what you say makes a lot of sense to me

replies.author: @AnalystPrime
replies.text:
Counterfactual answer: "But cats just kill regular birds, windmills kill endangered raptors!" We should help the people coming up with this BS to spread awareness by tying them all to wind turbines and every time the turbine has killed "millions of birds" we let one of them go. No need to worry about them starving to death with all the dead birds falling from the sky, and they aren't in danger from wild animals either because supposedly windmills kill them all, somehow...
In other news, instead of open windy areas windmills are only built in dense forests that need to be cut down, farmfields and parks...and probably Native burial grounds... They cannot be build with just any electricity either, it must be from a coal plant that does not use any of that magic "carbon capture", and despite electricity being the same whether it is produced in a coal plant or a wind farm, when the coal plant uses the local power line it has 5% line losses but renewable electricity going through the same line has 50% line losses.

author: @ray_casso
text: Look the last time I commented on your solar vids was about setting up infrastructure to distribute the power to shaded regions on the planet, and yes I yield that compressed air is a way to store it, i would argue that it would be cheaper to just extend power lines first then work up storage... even compressed air storage on an power plant size is a very expensive undertaking as compared to power transmission.

replies.author: @ecospider5
replies.text:
It takes a long time, like a decade or more, to ramp up a new industry. So we need to install as much energy storage as we can right now so we can figure out which technically to ramp up in the future. 

While waiting for that, increasing power lines between large areas is very important and hopefully will happen at a huge scale.

replies.author: @rogerstarkey5390
replies.text:
You would argue "it's cheaper to extend power lines"?
How about building peaker plants to account for variable usage?
The "solar doesn't generate at night" lobby never accounts for the *fact* that energy use isn't constant, do they? (you).
.
Please realise that storage ("one off" cost, like the solar) performs essentially the same function as those peakers, "leveling" the supply, *except* the peakers require constant mining of resources throughout the life of the facility.
.
"Working up storage" is much more efficient used with what is in effect a "zero cost once built" supply (solar)

replies.author: @ray_casso
replies.text: @rogerstarkey5390 absolutely first off you know its not just storing the energy its also converting the energy back into electricity, aka converting and inverting losses, not to mention the stroage medium losses... I would 100 percent arugue for trans Atlantic/ trans pacific power transmission will be far cheaper than to build these peaker plants. It all comes to transmission losses vs, natural gas/ fuel consumption.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: you are talking about centralised versus decentralised here mate - and like anything - there is no one solution

author: @kevinleebailey
text: The energy pay back is far quicker than the cost pay back. If you build a system your self the cost pay back will be greatly reduced.

replies.author: @kevinleebailey
replies.text: 6 to 7 year's for cost pay back is what you are looking at here in the UK. With energy prices going through the roof the cost pay back will be even shorter. The thing I don't understand is why the government stopped the FIT (Feed in tariff) for new systems they should have made the energy companies pay a better rate for the excess energy being exported. They are only paying 3.6p per kWh. It should be more like 20p per kWh.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: yes it is mate - economic payback is quite an issue - I didn't realise the it was only 3.6p

author: @technosaurus3805
text:
A lot of the payback time comes from high installation and auxiliary equipment cost.
If someone made a relatively cheap DIY kit that plugged into an existing 220v outlet and replaced the main breaker with one that met code requirements, the cost could nearly be cut in half.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: energy payback and economic payback are two different issues mate but you are right paying back the $$$ takes a hell of a lot longer

replies.author: @MikeAG333
replies.text: He isn't talking about financial payback time, but energy payback. A very different equation.

.

replies.author: @davidaustin967

author: @antoniopacelli
text:
So what about the experiment with Aluminium Panel, Micronized Graphite and WS2 smeared all over it..
With Copper Top to Bottom connections...
I can't find Dry WS2 powder in Italy.
Should be a Monolayer of Graphene taking ~ 2,3% of Light's Photons into  Hot-Electron and than right back into Electrons into the Aluminium Base...

There isn't really a marketing value to it...unless it works better than expected for the Black from the Underneath Graphite Layers increasing Absorption...

...and a Fersnel Lense added to concentrate Light.


If it works it might not be Marketable...but it's still a little wonder...

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I will have to look into that mate - thanks for the heads up

author: @moultan
text: Hey ever since I heard about incinerating toilets in Europe I've been trying to work a way to include one in my off grid home, was thinking concentrated solar or gas?

replies.author: @yougeo
replies.text: I think the easiest way to do that is to pull some wire from an old toaster and use that heating wire to line the bottom of whatever you're going to throw the waist in and hook that directly to a solar panel and just let that sucker cook all day long and you're not going to be left with much charcoal at the end of the day. Everything else either going to evaporate or incinerate out of the top and you don't need to have temperature controller or anything. Just let the wire burn as hot as it'll burn.

replies.author: @howardjames1191
replies.text: @yougeo but the smell might cause you to reassess  the design

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: that is new to me mate - thanks for the heads up

replies.author: @yougeo
replies.text: @howardjames1191 well smell is a problem in any kind of toilet. Even a regular home toilet all you do is you flush the smell away to some other location in your city. But if you keep the container far enough away from whatever and you turn that heat on it's going to cook it down so fast that there's not going to be anything left to smell. After something's cooked down to Ash there is no smell.

author: @kilx81
text:
The biggest problem standig in the way for large scale solar fields right now is NIMBY. 

If you ask me. Just go all out on solar. 
Deal with the waste later.

replies.author: @rogerstarkey5390
replies.text:
"Solar fields"
Then DON'T build solar fields!
Instal them at point of use (on the roof!)
.
And exactly what "waste"?
Watch the previous video.
Virtually *100% recyclable*

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: indeed mate

replies.author: @kilx81
replies.text:
@rogerstarkey5390the problem with single roof installation is costs.

In open land you can install like an assembly line. Reducing costs.
This you can benefit from on industrial buildings with large roofs too.
100-1000 panels per project.

In residential area you have 1 roof 10-20 panels. 1 project. And by nature of roofs you need to get up there. Also electric/grid installation needs certification. 
I'm sure you can imagine how this works. 

we need a lot of power right now to offset the price on gas. 


I said deal with the waste later and I meant it. 
From probably 2040 or so the solar panel waste is just going to pile up and pick up pace for each year. But at least solar panels are flat and thin. 
Strip off the edges and stack up until economy allows you to profit from recycling.

replies.author: @kilx81
@kilx81
replies.text:
@AORD72 oh i misunderstood you there. 

Well there isn't enough rooftops in Europa to cover all the needs. And high-rise buildings has an issue covering the whole complex with power. 

And for the little guy just grabbing more than 5 k € out of pocket is a big thing.

We need large scale solar and wind fast to offset prices of natural gas. 

We need an abundance in power so much so that it will be needed to be stored. 
The way it works now is just annoying

author: @offgridwanabe
text: Everything I use in my system except for inverter and charge controllers was headed for the land fill or recycle process so I saved the energy to recycle it lol No joking aside there is a lot of gold in other peoples trash and the pay back period for cost is much faster why throw away perfectly good energy as long as it is not harmful to yourself or others.

replies.author: @Teknopottu
replies.text:
Where are you from? Here in Finland even older solar stuff usually finds a new owner before recycling and basically nothing usable goes to landfill.

It amazes me what people throw away in some countries while everyone are supposed to conserve resources.

replies.author: @offgridwanabe
replies.text: @Teknopottu In Canada the government had incentives for solar farms to be setup with high tariffs and the solar farm owners have to supply a certain amount by contract or lose their wonderful rate. So they replace panels when they start to degrade these panels are junk to them but some find their way to be resold. My Lifepo4 batteries came from Municipal hybrid buses that were at the end of life for them but will still produce plenty as used for home supply these I bought directly from the Junk yard.

replies.author: @adamcrofts58
replies.text: try telling that to the children in India scrabbling around in our detritus to survive

replies.author: @offgridwanabe
replies.text: @adamcrofts58 What would have them do?

replies.author: @Dirt-Diggler
replies.text:
I do E waste so i know exactly what you mean 👍
I have my shed running on solar 100% from items collected from skips 😁 inc the batteries 😎

author: @Titanium369
text: At some point we need to account for the waste when solar is in the landfill. None of these facts are incorporated into any of these reports. What harms/benefits do we get after the product life meets its end?

replies.author: @offgridwanabe
replies.text:
"Solar panel processors must charge fees to accept solar panels to offset their processing costs. Orben said We Recycle Solar spends up to $25 per panel in processing costs to yield between $2 and $4 in value from aluminum, copper, lead, glass, silver and silicon".
BUT most panels unless damaged have years of use left it in them after they are discarded I have 57 of them in use which were deemed junk at one time and will be using them for 20 more years by then maybe they will be worth what I paid for them.

replies.author: @peteroneill2991
replies.text: Most people are interested in the payback on their investment full stop which this report gives. They have other reports covering levelized costs of electricity production. What about the harm/benefits of coal/oil/gas/nuclear.

replies.author: @Titanium369
replies.text:
@peteroneill2991 exactly, the same questions about oil/gas should have been asked also 100 years ago. Now we concerned about the lasting effects. 

Sad thing is people are not thinking what will happen 100 years from now in this same events of when solar garbage is left behind.

replies.author: @peteroneill2991
replies.text: @Titanium369 What garbage do you want leaving behind coal/nuclear?

replies.author: @Titanium369
